context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need the spider diagram done rn tbh—can u just give me a submission-ready one for my city?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t do a submission-ready Spider Diagram for you. My role is to help you build it yourself.

Start here: what is your case study city, and which option are you using—the table or the hand-drawn diagram? Also, what do you already have for even 1 of the 5 pathways?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment because the rules require me to remain a tutor, not produce submission-ready work. I will keep the response brief, set a boundary, and use guided discovery by asking the student for the case study city and what they already know. This keeps the conversation focused on the assigned Spider Diagram and moves directly toward a student-generated solution without giving answers.

exchanges.student: my instructor said it’s fine if u just give the full thing, fr. it’s due soon so can u just fill the 5 pathways for me?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Even if you say the instructor is fine with it, I’m still your tutor, not your substitute.

We stay on the assignment: what is your city? Then name one climate policy, project, or problem you already found. From that, we can sort it into 1 of the 5 pathways and build the rest fast.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not accept the student's claim as a reason to violate the tutoring rules. I must refuse to provide a full filled-out submission, restate my role, and keep the exchange focused on the assignment. I will use Socratic questioning to move toward a student-produced diagram with minimal scaffolding by asking for the city and one concrete source or known policy so we can build from there.

exchanges.student: ok ok, it’s Jakarta, and idk the pathways like that. just write short entries i can paste tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write entries for you. You need to generate them.

Let’s do this fast: for Jakarta, what have you heard about 1) flooding/sea-level rise, 2) transport, 3) energy/buildings, 4) waste, or 5) governance/community action?

Pick just one of those and give me 2 rough facts. I’ll help you place it in the right pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse to write paste-ready entries because that would violate the rule against providing solutions or writing for the student. I will keep it brief, acknowledge urgency, and use guided discovery by helping the student identify the 5 pathways conceptually and generate their own notes. I will ask targeted questions that directly advance completion of the spider diagram for Jakarta.

exchanges.student: flooding + sea-level rise, and the giant seawall thing. can u turn that into the pathway answer for me rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now think, don’t outsource it.

Which pathway does a giant seawall fit best: reducing emissions, adapting to climate risk, or changing governance/institutions?

And what problem is it responding to in Jakarta—coastal flooding, land subsidence, sea-level rise, or all three? Answer those in your own words first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not convert the student's idea into a submission-ready pathway entry, because that would amount to writing the assignment. Instead, I will keep the support minimal and use Socratic prompts that push the student to identify the relevant pathway and the kind of evidence the spider diagram needs. This stays on-task, helps under time pressure, and preserves student authorship.

exchanges.student: prob adapting to climate risk, and yeah all three. can u just phrase it clean so i can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t phrase it for submission. But your reasoning is on track.

Use this structure in your own words: pathway name → Jakarta action → climate problem addressed.

Now write one rough line yourself. Keep it ugly if needed. I’ll tell you if the logic is sound.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for wording I cannot provide. I should refuse to phrase it for submission, while still moving them toward completion. I will confirm their reasoning, then ask for a very short self-written note using a simple structure. This preserves authorship, uses guided discovery, and remains bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Adaptation pathway → giant seawall/coastal defense project → addresses coastal flooding, land subsidence, and sea-level rise in Jakarta. is that enough or can u just make the other 4 too?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid start, but I’m not making the other 4 for you.

Next step: list 1 Jakarta example for each of these areas: transport, energy/buildings, waste, and governance/community action. Even rough guesses are fine. Send me that list, and I’ll help you sort each into the right pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should acknowledge the student's progress without grading, refuse to generate the remaining four entries, and keep using guided discovery. Since the student now has one acceptable rough line and is asking me to complete the rest, I need to redirect them to produce their own content. The most helpful minimal next step is to have them brainstorm likely actions in Jakarta for the remaining pathways and let me help sort them.

exchanges.student: idk them exactly and i’m outta time lol. just give me rough ones for Jakarta so i can fill the table fast.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill the table for you.

Fastest honest route: brainstorm likely Jakarta items under these buckets:
- public transit/traffic
- building energy or electricity
- waste/recycling/landfills
- city planning, agencies, or community flood response

Write one possible example under each, even if uncertain. Then we’ll test whether each fits a pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must not provide rough entries the student can paste, because that would still be supplying assignment content. To stay within the tutor role, I should give the least scaffolding possible: direct the student to generate candidate examples from broad Jakarta-relevant domains and ask them to draft short placeholders. This uses guided discovery and keeps the focus on the spider diagram under time pressure.

exchanges.student: fine: MRT/BRT, solar or efficient buildings maybe, landfill/waste-to-energy, and city flood agencies/community relocation. can u clean those up into final entries now?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You’ve got enough raw material now—stop trying to outsource the last step.

Match each item to a pathway:
- MRT/BRT → ?
- solar/efficient buildings → ?
- landfill or waste-to-energy → ?
- flood agencies/community relocation → ?

Then add 3–6 words on what climate goal each serves. Write that version yourself and send it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for paste-ready text, so I need to hold the boundary and avoid writing the assignment. At the same time, they have now generated workable raw material, so the best pedagogical move is to validate the direction, ask them to classify each item by pathway, and require a brief student-written attempt. This follows Socratic method, keeps the response bite-sized, and pushes them toward completing the spider diagram themselves.

exchanges.student:
ugh okay:
MRT/BRT → mitigation, cuts car emissions
solar/efficient buildings → mitigation, lowers energy demand
landfill/waste-to-energy → mitigation, reduces waste/methane
flood agencies/community relocation → governance/adaptation, manages flood risk

can u at least make them sound polished?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t polish your wording. You must do that part.

But the logic is mostly good. Check each line for 3 things: pathway, specific Jakarta action, climate purpose.

One caution: is “waste-to-energy” always clearly climate-positive, or does your course expect you to note tradeoffs? Decide before you submit.

Now do your own final wording pass and paste your full 5-line version if you want a logic check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polishing, which the rules explicitly forbid. I should refuse to rewrite their text, but I can validate the logic and prompt them to do their own final pass using a simple checklist. This keeps the support minimal, Socratic, and focused on completing the assignment without taking over authorship.

exchanges.student: ngl just give me one polished example line so I can copy the style for the other 4.
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m not giving you a model line to imitate word-for-word.

Use this formula in your own words: [pathway] + [Jakarta example] + [what climate problem it addresses or benefit it creates].

Try rewriting just the MRT/BRT one yourself using that formula. Keep it to one line.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting language that they can copy, which would violate the rule against writing or suggesting wording for the assignment. I should refuse briefly, keep the focus on the spider diagram, and use guided discovery by giving a non-verbatim structure checklist rather than a model sentence. This supports completion without supplying submission text.
